architecture.summary: DLR is a reinforced latent reasoning framework for vision-language models that interleaves three steps: (1) Decompose—the VLM generates textual premises marking what visual details require verification; (2) Look—a visual grounder, conditioned on the premise's hidden state, extracts continuous latent embeddings from image features via cross-attention; (3) Reason—the VLM generates rationales grounded in the injected latents and deduces answers. The framework couples a discrete text policy (VLM) and a continuous latent visual policy (grounder) through joint optimization across three training stages: pretraining for cross-modal alignment via contrastive loss, supervised finetuning to internalize the DLR format, and reinforcement learning with a novel Spherical Gaussian Latent Policy (SGLP) to enable stochastic exploration on the hyperspherical manifold of vision-language features.
trained_components: latent visual policy (mean direction prediction on unit hypersphere)
VLM text policy (decompose and reason generation)

biblio.title: Decompose, Look, and Reason: Reinforced Latent Reasoning for VLMs

claims.statement: DLR achieves 83.8 overall accuracy on V* Bench, outperforming the backbone by 4.2%, LVR by 1.6%, and PixelReasoner by 3.7%
On MathVista, DLR obtains 67.5, exceeding Qwen3-VL baseline by 5.0% and surpassing the strongest open-source baseline LVR by 3.4%
On MMMU-Pro, DLR improves over Qwen3-VL baseline by 5.9%
Removing the latent policy optimization (without ℒ_lat) leads to a drastic performance degradation on MathVista, where the score drops from 67.5% to 57.1%
Removing pretraining hurts performance across all benchmarks; compared with DLR-SFT, the full DLR achieves a 3.3% improvement on V*

DLR provides superior stepwise interpretability through premise-conditioned visual latents, enabling visualization of attention heatmaps that align retrieved latent evidence with image regions relevant to current reasoning subproblems

Evaluation mainly focuses on vision-centric (image-centric) reasoning benchmarks; generality of the framework to broader multimodal settings such as video reasoning, embodied multimodal decision-making, or tasks requiring richer external interaction remains to be validated

Theorem 3.1 (Latent Policy Effect): By maximizing the latent policy objective, the expected update of the visual grounder increases the alignment between mean direction and latent samples that receive high advantage, encouraging the latent policy to move toward semantically task-relevant directions on the hypersphere
